summary:
Timbuktu (Tombouctou) was founded around 1100 AD as a seasonal camp by Tuareg nomads on the southern edge of the Sahara Desert at a bend of the Niger River in what is now Mali, growing from a commercial waypoint into one of the most important cities in medieval Africa — a crossroads of trans-Saharan trade, Islamic scholarship, and sub-Saharan agricultural wealth. The city's position made it uniquely valuable: it was the northernmost point where river trade from the forest zones could meet camel caravan routes crossing the Sahara, making it a natural exchange point where gold and salt, kola nuts and slaves, manuscripts and scholars converged. Under the [[event:93|Mali Empire]] in the 14th century, and especially during [[event:2256|Mansa Musa's]] reign, Timbuktu began its transformation into a major Islamic scholarly center.

Timbuktu reached its greatest prosperity and intellectual glory under the [[event:2259|Songhai Empire]] in the 15th–16th centuries, when the [[event:2257|Sankore mosque-university]] attracted scholars from across the Islamic world and the city's libraries housed an estimated 700,000 manuscripts on theology, law, mathematics, astronomy, history, and literature — the largest collection of written knowledge in sub-Saharan Africa and one of the great intellectual repositories of the medieval world. At its peak, Timbuktu's population reached 100,000–115,000, of whom approximately 25,000 were students. The city's legendary wealth — filtered through European imagination as a place of inexhaustible gold, giving rise to expressions like 'from here to Timbuktu' meaning an impossibly distant, fabulously wealthy place — derived from its role as the pivot of the gold-salt trade. The Moroccan invasion of 1591 ended Timbuktu's golden age, scattering its scholars and disrupting the trade networks that sustained it.
